summary: Gruber argues that Substack's latest $100 million fundraise at a $1.1 billion valuation is unjustified given the company's fundamentals. He dissects a New York Times interview with Substack's cofounders, noting that their pivot toward competing with YouTube rather than Mailchimp contradicts Substack's founding identity as a writer-focused platform. Through back-of-the-envelope math comparing Substack's per-subscriber revenue (50-70 cents/month) to the New York Times's business, he concludes Substack is nowhere near worth one-eighth of the Times. He contends that a 10 percent commission on newsletter subscriptions could sustain a nice business but not a billion-dollar one, and that needing another $100 million at this stage signals the opposite of validation.
key_points: Substack's simple 10% commission model should already be profitable if it actually worked as described — needing $100M more proves it doesn't
Substack's cofounders now say they're more interested in competing with YouTube than Mailchimp, contradicting the writer-focused identity that attracted their talent
At 50-70 cents per subscriber per month, Substack's unit economics don't support a $1 billion valuation compared to the NYT's $8.5 billion market cap with 11 million subscribers
A higher valuation from investors doesn't validate a strategy — only actual profits and profitable growth would do that
Substack has no advertising business and many writers chose it specifically because it has no ads, creating a strategic tension with growth ambitions
money_quotes: If their business model were actually as simple as described, they'd already be profitable and wouldn't have needed to raise another $100 million.
This could be like when a guy who just lost every dollar in his pocket playing blackjack withdraws a few more grand from the ATM in the casino.
What would validate Substack's strategy is showing proof of actual profits and profitable growth.
Does a company 'more interested in taking on YouTube than MailChimp' sound like a company focused on writers as talent and readers as users to you?
So Substack only pockets like 50–70 cents per month from subscribers, per publication.
I firmly believe one could build a very nice business taking 10 percent of subscription revenue for a blogging/newsletter platform. I do not think that's a $1 billion business, though.
But what do I know, other than running a profitable independent website for the last 20 or so years?
key_insight: Substack's need to raise another $100 million after years of operation and a roster of popular writers reveals that their simple commission-based business model cannot sustain a billion-dollar valuation, and their pivot toward social media features betrays the writer-focused identity that built their brand.
